parsed.summary: The cumulative v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff introduces critical new infrastructure—retry/backoff, BigModel error dispatch, job lifecycle changes—without dedicated test coverage for the highest-risk module (glm-client.mjs, 155 lines changed). The integration surface between retry.mjs, bigmodel-errors.mjs, and glm-client.mjs is untested at the unit level. Given that every user operation flows through this client, a regression in auth handling, request construction, or error classification would be invisible until production runtime. This is a blocking gap for a release targeting a sampling-sweep validation milestone.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs has 155 lines of changes with no dedicated test file
findings.body: The core API client module (scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs) shows 155 lines of changes across the v0.4.0→v0.4.6 diff, integrating the new retry.mjs backoff engine and bigmodel-errors.mjs error dispatch. However, no tests/glm-client.test.mjs exists in the changed files list. While retry.mjs (352-line test) and bigmodel-errors.mjs (189-line test) are individually tested, the integration layer that decides when to retry vs. fail vs. map errors is the glue between them and lives in glm-client.mjs. This means: (1) authentication header construction after a retry reconnection isn't verified, (2) the mapping of raw BigModel HTTP errors to the new error taxonomy in bigmodel-errors.mjs isn't tested end-to-end, (3) request body reconstruction on retry (e.g., re-reading streams, re-serializing payloads) has no coverage. This is the single most critical path in the plugin.
findings.recommendation: Add a tests/glm-client.test.mjs covering at minimum: (1) successful request/response cycle, (2) retry on transient error with backoff integration, (3) non-retryable error classification, (4) auth header presence across retries, (5) timeout handling. Mock the HTTP layer to avoid external dependencies.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs has 78 lines of changes without dedicated test coverage
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs shows 78 lines of changes but no corresponding tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs appears in the diff. Job tracking is responsible for preventing duplicate work, maintaining state consistency across concurrent operations, and supporting the --wait/--background execution modes added in v0.4.5. Race conditions in job state transitions (e.g., marking a job complete while another process is polling) would manifest as lost work or hung processes—failures that are hard to diagnose in production. The existing tests/job-render.test.mjs may exercise render paths but likely doesn't test job lifecycle state transitions directly.
findings.recommendation: Add tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs covering: (1) job creation and state transitions (pending→running→complete/failed), (2) concurrent access patterns (two processes tracking the same job), (3) cleanup of stale jobs, (4) --wait polling behavior on a job that transitions states.

findings.title: scripts/lib/process.mjs deleted without visible import migration verification
findings.body: The diff stat shows scripts/lib/process.mjs with 67 lines removed and no replacement. While scripts/check-imports.mjs was modified (5 lines), which likely updates import validation, there is no automated test confirming that zero remaining imports reference the deleted module. A missed import would cause a runtime MODULE_NOT_FOUND error that only surfaces when the affected code path is executed—potentially in an uncommon error-handling branch or edge case not hit during UAT scenarios A–I.
findings.recommendation: Verify that `grep -r "from.*['\"].*process\.mjs" scripts/` returns zero hits. Confirm scripts/check-imports.mjs now rejects any import of process.mjs. Consider adding a CI gate that fails if any .mjs file imports from a module that doesn't exist on disk.

next_steps: Add tests/glm-client.test.mjs before proceeding with the v0.4.7 sweep. This is the highest-ROI test gap—155 lines of untested critical-path code.
Run `grep -rn "process\.mjs" scripts/` to confirm the deleted module has no remaining importers.

Verify that the v0.4.5 UAT scenarios G and I (new in v0.4.5, not present in v0.4.4) actually exercise the retry/backoff paths added in v0.4.6, since those UAT tests were likely written before the retry integration.
Consider whether tracked-jobs state transitions need atomicity guarantees (e.g., file locking) for the --background mode where multiple processes may interact with the same job state file.
